September 9, 1981 
Robert Stern, Esq. 
General Counsel 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Dear Bob: 
Re: 
Request for Advisory Opinion on 
Filing Requirements of General 
Purpose Committee 
As we discussed recently, the City of Berkeley 
has not requested an Advisory Opinion on this question. 
Consequently on behalf of the California Real Estate 
Political Action Committee, and other committees similarly 
situated and, pursuant to Government Code §83114, and 
2 Cal Admin §18320, we respectfully request that the 
Commission clarify the filing requirements of "general pur-
pose" campaign committees as described below. 
Amendments to the Political Reform Act of 1974, 
adopted in 1980 and effective January 1, 1981, modified 
Government Code §84200 and §84215 to "categorize" commit-
tees by their type of activity and thereby regulate the 
frequency and location of campaign reports to be filed by 
such committees. 
One purpose of these amendments was to 
specify a "general purpose committee" whose activity was 
not confined to a specific candidate, ballot measure or 
election and allow and require such committee to file on 
statutory known dates each year and to file reports in 
prescribed locations. 
This amendment deleted the previous 
law where such committees with considerable activity might 
file numerous and frequent copies of their reports or 
abbreviated statements in many local jurisdictions. 
It is our understanding that the legislature 
ing the "general purpose" requirements and 
ng deadlines, "thresholds", and prescribed loca-
Leempted conflicting filing requirements of local 
nts, leaving Government Code §81013 to apply to 
Lal purpose committees. 
SACRAMENTO OFFICE -
SUITE 550. 1225 EIGHTH STREET. SACRAMENTO. CA 95814 
1916) 446-6752 
LOS ANGELES OFFICE -
SUITE 309. 612 SOUTH FLOWER STREET. LOS ANGELES. CA 90017 
1213) 628-5867 
NEWPORT BEACH OFFICE -
SUITE 4000. 4000 MACARTHUR BOULEVARD. NEWPORT BEACH. CA 92660 
1714) 752-9318 


Robert Stern, 
q. 
September 3, 1981 
Page Two 
We have had a number of communications with 
such cities as Berkeley, Santa Monica and Newport Beach 
where their local officials believe that their filing 
requirements, now very much skewed from the state filing 
deadlines, are applicable to general purpose committees 
notwithstanding the fact that these local deadlines impose 
unreasonable and confusing additional filing requirements. 
This problem is serious in that there are a 
number of cities with such conflicting statutes (see 
Exhibi t A). 
"[Ale respectfully request that the Commission 
clarify the scope of the 1980 amendments as to preemption 
of such local ordinances, publish your conclusion in your 
next revision of your Campaign Manual and notify the 
cities. 
matter. 
VGN/ds 
Enclosure 
We appreciate your time and interest in this 
Very truly yours, 
Jr. 


"EXHIBIT A" 
The following ordinances give examples of 
additional, conflicting and overlapping filing require-
ments for "general purpose" committees, including: 
1. 
different deadlines (40-12-65 day plus 
an additional pre election deadline) 
2. 
filing with Clerk of City 
3. 
registering with Clerk of City 
BERKELEY (Ordinance 4700 N.S.) 
Reports are required to be filed with the 
City Clerk twice before and once following a Berkeley 
election on the 40-12-65 day filing schedule. 
(§410) 
Additionally, each committee (contributes $250 to 
Berkeley campaign) must file a Statement of Organization 
with the City Clerk of Berkeley within ten days after 
its formation. 
(§402) 
CONCORD (Municipal Code Article IV Chapter 9(1)) 
Must file Statement of Organization with the 
City Clerk within ten days after a committee has been 
formed. 
(§4910) 
CORONADO (Chapter 2.80 Municipal Code) 
Filings are made with the City Clerk. 
DAVIS (Ordinance 745) 
Required to file campaign statements with the 
City Clerk on the 40-12-65 schedule plus on the Friday 
preceeding the election date. 
(§8A-4A) 
DEL MA.R (Article VII, Municipal Code) 
Filings are required on a 40-12-65 day schedule 
with the city Clerk. 
(§2-83-(d)) 
Additionally, a State-
ment of Organization must be filed with the City Clerk 
within ten days of committee formation or upon any cam-
paign contribution. 
(§2-83 (c)) 
Clerk. 
FREMONT (Chapter 1.4 Municipal Code) 
Reports are required to be filed with the City 
(§2-1402) 
NEWPORT BEACH (Chapter 1. 25 Municipal Code) 
Each Committee files four cumulative campaign 
statements with the city Clerk. 
(1.25.040) 


"EXHIBIT A" 
Page Two 
SAN JOSE (Ordinance 20338) 
Each committee is required to file three cumula-
tive itemized statements with the City Clerk. 
(§2.2l.070) 
SAN LUIS OBISPO (Article II, Chapter 8, 
Municipal Code) 
Reports are required to be filed with the 
City Clerk and an additional campaign statement is due on 
the Thursday prior to the election. 
(§2806) 
(§11208) 
SANTA MONICA (Article XI, Chapter I, Municipal 
Code) 
Reports must be filed with the City Clerk.

sections.question: Whether amendments to the Political Reform Act effective January 1, 1981, which established standardized filing requirements for general purpose committees active in more than one jurisdiction, preempt local ordinances that impose additional or different filing requirements for such committees.
sections.conclusion: The Act does not preempt local filing requirements as a general matter. However, if local requirements become so burdensome that compliance would not be permitted by Government Code Section 81013, they may be invalid. Such determinations must be made on a case-by-case basis, requiring specific showing of statutory hardship or confusion caused by a particular local requirement.
sections.question_synthetic: Do state amendments establishing standardized filing dates and locations for general purpose committees preempt conflicting local filing requirements imposed by cities like Berkeley, Santa Monica, and Newport Beach?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that state law does not automatically preempt local filing requirements for general purpose committees, but case-by-case analysis is required if a local requirement creates substantial hardship or confusion that violates Section 81013.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains significant OCR degradation but structure is identifiable. Includes both incoming request (September 9, 1981) and FPPC response (January 28, 1982). Response letter clearly marked with Q/C sections identifiable despite OCR errors. Exhibit A attached to incoming request provides detailed list of conflicting local ordinances. Document date appears to be 1982 based on response date.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether amendments to the Political Reform Act effective January 1, 1981, which established standardized filing requirements for general purpose committees active in more than one jurisdiction, preempt local ordinances that impose additional or different filing requirements for such committees.

CONCLUSION: The Act does not preempt local filing requirements as a general matter. However, if local requirements become so burdensome that compliance would not be permitted by Government Code Section 81013, they may be invalid. Such determinations must be made on a case-by-case basis, requiring specific showing of statutory hardship or confusion caused by a particular local requirement.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to an inquiry about whether 1980 amendments to the Political Reform Act preempt local ordinances imposing additional filing requirements on general purpose committees. The FPPC advises that preemption is not automatic but requires case-by-case analysis based on whether compliance would create statutory violations or substantial hardship.
